coredata.dc:title: A novel detection method for sulfur content in ship fuel based on metal-doped tin oxide quantum dots as fluorescent sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jianqiao
Li, Wenping
Li, Hongjin
Zhang, Yanan
Zhai, Zhaoxia
Wang, Yang
Fu, Ce
Wang, Junsheng
coredata.dc:description: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) issues compulsory regulations that require fuel sulfur content (FSC) less than 0.50% (m/m) to reduce sulfur dioxide emission from ships. However, current FSC detection suffers from low efficiency and high cost. In this work, a novel method for FSC detection is developed based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). The adsorption properties of primary functional groups -SH, –COOH, –NH2, –OH, and –CN of organic compounds in fuel on pristine and metal-doped tin oxide (SnO2) QDs are simulated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Ni dopant is selected after screening adsorption of organics on metal-doped SnO2. The fluorescent sensor is designed by nickel-doped SnO2 (Ni-SnO2) QDs for the FSC detection. The detection range and limit of detection are 0.429–0.97% m/m and 0.065% m/m, respectively. Furthermore, the sensing mechanism is discussed and the quenching of fluorescence emission of Ni-SnO2 QDs is ascribed to the photoinduced electron transfer. This work provides a novel technique based on Ni-doped SnO2 QDs for actual fast-responsive, highly-selective, reliably-repeatable, low-cost FSC detection, which can meet the detection requirements of FSC below 0.10% m/m in the emission control areas (ECAs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fuel sulfur content detection
First principle calculations
Adsorption energy
metal-doped SnO2
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of pristine SnO2 QDs
Experimental characterizations of pristine and Ni-modified SnO2 QDs
Fluorescence response of Ni-SnO2 QDs to FSC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Liaoning Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the Liaoning Applied Fundamental Research Project (Grant No. 2022JH2/101300158 and 2023JH2/101300014 ), the Scientific Research Funding Project of Educational Department of Liaoning Province (Grant No. LJKZ0060 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 3132023235 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescent spectra of pristine SnO2 QDs in the presence of increasing FSC; (b) The correlation between the fluorescence response (F0/Fn) at 311 nm against FSC.
XRD patterns of pristine and Ni-modified SnO2 QDs.
HRTEM images of (a) SnO2 QDs and (b) Ni-SnO2 QDs in aqueous solution; HAADF-STEM image of (c) Ni-SnO2 QDs and elemental mapping: (d) Ni (e) O and (f) Sn.
XPS spectra of pristine SnO2 QDs and Ni-SnO2 QDs: (a) survey; (b) Sn 3d; (c) O 1 s; (d) Ni 2p.
EDS spectra of SnO2 QDs and Ni-SnO2 QDs.
Optical properties of pristine SnO2 QDs and Ni-SnO2 QDs: (a) UV–Vis spectra and Tauc plots and (b) Fluorescence emission spectra.
(a) Fluorescent spectra of Ni-SnO2 QDs in the presence of increasing FSC; (b) The correlation of fluorescence response against FSC with the calibration curve for FSC detection.

(a) Fluorescence lifetimes of Ni-SnO2 before and after the addition of diluted fuel, (b) UV–Vis spectra of Ni-SnO2 before and after the addition of diluted fuel, (c) The UV–Vis spectra of diluted fuel (blue line) and the fluorescence spectra of Ni-SnO2 QDs (red line) and (d) valence-band XPS spectra of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Adsorption energies of CH3SH, CH3COOH, CH3NH2, CH3OH and CH3CN on SnO2 (110) surface and metal-doped SnO2 (110) surface.
Mulliken atomic population of CH3SH, CH3COOH, CH3NH2, CH3OH, and CH3CN before and after adsorption.
Detection results and recoveries of FSC detected by Ni-SnO2.
Comparison of the LOD of FSC between Ni-SnO2 QDs and other reported research results.
HOMO/LUMO of organics relative to the normal hydrogen electrode.

head.ce:title: A novel detection method for sulfur content in ship fuel based on metal-doped tin oxide quantum dots as fluorescent sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information Science and Technology, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Information Science and Technology, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, Liaoning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Microfluidic Optoelectronic Sensing, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Microfluidic Optoelectronic Sensing, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Information Science and Technology, Dalian Maritime University, Linghai Road 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116026, Liaoning, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) issues compulsory regulations that require fuel sulfur content (FSC) less than 0.50% (m/m) to reduce sulfur dioxide emission from ships. However, current FSC detection suffers from low efficiency and high cost. In this work, a novel method for FSC detection is developed based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). The adsorption properties of primary functional groups -SH, –COOH, –NH2, –OH, and –CN of organic compounds in fuel on pristine and metal-doped tin oxide (SnO2) QDs are simulated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Ni dopant is selected after screening adsorption of organics on metal-doped SnO2. The fluorescent sensor is designed by nickel-doped SnO2 (Ni-SnO2) QDs for the FSC detection. The detection range and limit of detection are 0.429–0.97% m/m and 0.065% m/m, respectively. Furthermore, the sensing mechanism is discussed and the quenching of fluorescence emission of Ni-SnO2 QDs is ascribed to the photoinduced electron transfer. This work provides a novel technique based on Ni-doped SnO2 QDs for actual fast-responsive, highly-selective, reliably-repeatable, low-cost FSC detection, which can meet the detection requirements of FSC below 0.10% m/m in the emission control areas (ECAs).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel is the most crucial energy source during marine transportation at present. Ship fuel is the mixture with compositions such as gasoline, kerosene, and diesel fuel [1]. From the elemental aspect, ship fuel is comprised of C, H, O, N and S, with trace metal elements [2]. The combustion of ship fuel will produce gaseous pollutants such as CO2, SO2 and NO2. According to the assessment from International Maritime Organization (IMO), SO2 emission from ships accounts for 13% of the annual global emissions [3]. Meanwhile, IMO has issued compulsory global regulations on the fuel sulfur content (FSC) in ship fuel, which is restricted below 0.50% m/m by 2020 [4]. Therefore, FSC detection is an essential technique required by maritime authorities, shipping companies and fuel supplier.

The inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5] and the sector field inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (SF-ICP-MS) [6] have been used to detect FSC during the past few decades. These traditional detection methods are time-consuming and low in efficiency. From the consideration of real-time detection, these techniques are not able to meet the requirements of actual regulatory. For the real-time monitoring of exhaust emission, the “sniffing” method [7], optical remote-sensing technology [8], and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [9] are widely used to detect FSC. However, the techniques of sniffing and optical remote-sensing could usually be affected by weather conditions, speed of ships, and the marine environment. Facilities for X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy are expensive and have to be operated by proficient technicians. Therefore, it is urgently expected to develop a fast and sensitive FSC detection technique with low cost.

The fluorescent sensors are attractive because they have excellent capacity in the detection of trace target substance. They are less susceptible to external interferences due to direct interaction with targets, circumventing the impacts induced by influencing factors that may be involved into other detection methods. Thus, fluorescence sensors exhibit exceptional sensitivity and selectivity, enabling the detection of weak signals. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have excellent optical properties, such as wide absorption, narrow emission, high quantum efficiency and outstanding stability [10]. Therefore, they have been usually applied to the fluorescent sensor. Recently, Liang developed a MoS2/CdTe-based dual-signal fluorescent sensor for tetracycline detection in milk with the detection range of 0.1–1 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) of 6.74 nM [11]. Zhang synthesized multifunctional carbon dots and it was used to detect Fe3+ and thiol [12]. Liu prepared SnO2 QDs as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions, with the linear range of 10-2-105 µM and the LOD of 5 nM [13]. The principle of fluorescent sensor are the quenching, recovery and enhancement of photoluminescence [14]. The natures of QDs are important factors during the fluorescent detection. Nanostructured semiconductors such as SnO2 [13], carbon dots [15], CdTe [11], CdS [16], and CdSe [17] are popular in the development of fluorescent sensors. However, the drawbacks of complicated synthesis, instability and environmental toxicity inhibit their practical applications.

Among these materials, SnO2 QDs hold the characteristics of excellent stability, low cost, environmental friendliness and simple synthesis process [18]. Gu used the fluorescence enhancement of SnO2 QDs to detect DNA hybridization with the LOD of 10 fM [19]. Europium-doped SnO2 were prepared as nanostructures to detect Cd2+ ions in drinking water [20]. Upasana Choudhari prepared Co and Fe-doped SnO2 and it exhibited excellent selectivity toward Cd(II) and Cr(VI) ions compared to other heavy metal ions [21]. Mrudul Modak synthesized N-doped SnO2-rGO nanohybrids for NO2 detection with high sensitivity, selectivity and stability [22]. Therefore, SnO2 QDs are of great potential to be developed as fluorescent sensors for the fast, sensitive, low-cost detection of FSC.

One of the difficulties in the FSC detection is the complex composition of ship fuel, which contains more than 1000 compounds. It is time-consuming and impossible to learn the interaction between the SnO2 nanostructure and every compound. The first-principle calculation based on density functional theory (DFT) could be an efficient tool to study the adsorption properties of organics on semiconductor surface. For instance, Guo split the complex dye molecule into simple molecules and calculated the adsorption energy on the Pt-doped SnO2 surface by DFT calculations [23]. The results of first principle-based calculation could be beneficial to predict the properties of fluorescent sensors.

In this work, SnO2 QDs are attempted to detect FSC in ship fuel based on the principle of fluorescent sensors. The adsorption energy of primary functional groups of -SH, –COOH, –NH2, –OH, and –CN on pristine and metal-doped SnO2 QDs are simulated by DFT calculations. The metal element that is able to enhance the interaction of sulfide with SnO2 is screened and selected for the modification of SnO2. Ni-doped SnO2 QDs are synthesized by hydrothermal method, and their crystal structure and optical properties are characterized. The fluorescent sensor is designed by Ni-SnO2 to detect FSC and the sensing mechanism is further discussed.

The details of materials and methods are available in supplementary materials, which include computational methodology, materials and reagents, preparation of pristine and Ni-doped SnO2 QDs, experimental characterizations as well as fluorescence detection of sulfur content by semiconductor QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of pristine SnO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectra of pristine SnO2 QDs before and after the fuel incorporation are shown in Fig. 1
(a). The peaks centered at 311 nm are assigned to SnO2 QDs and a new fluorescence peak emerges in the range of 370–420 nm, assigned to the organic compounds in fuel. As FSC raised from 0.429 to 0.97% m/m, the SnO2 peak gradually decreases because the fluorescence quenching caused by incorporated fuel. Meanwhile, the fuel peak increases in intensity. The general monotonous relationship between fluorescence response and FSC can be observed, as shown in Fig. 1 (b). However, the correlation is too implicit to establish a calibration curve for FSC detection. The influence of sulfides on fluorescence emission would be concealed by other substances in fuel, which interact with SnO2 QDs simultaneously. Therefore, necessary modification on SnO2 QDs is expected to enhance its selectivity to sulfur-containing compounds.

As the fluorescence response reflects the impacts of external substances on semiconductors, the adsorption properties of these species on SnO2 QDs are crucial and decisive in the detection process of fluorescent probe technique. According to the calculation of surface energies of SnO2, it is found that the (110) surface exhibits the lowest energy compared to other surfaces, being the most stable surface [24]. Consequently, the (110) surface is selected as the typical surface in the calculation of adsorption of organics on SnO2 surfaces, as shown in Fig. S1 (a). The primary compounds in fuel consist of elements such as C, H, O, N, S and trace amounts of metal elements. There are many types of organic compounds with complex structures, such as fluorene, phenanthrene, anthracene, thiophene, quinoline, porphyrin and thiol [25,26]. Among these components, the thiol is the predominant sulfide. Due to the complexity of substances in fuel, it is not possible to study all of them in an exhaustive way. Therefore, the thiol is selected as the typical representative of sulfide. For the same reason, the simple molecule structures of CH3SH, CH3COOH, CH3NH2, CH3OH, and CH3CN are selected as representatives of possible adsorbates in fuel on semiconductor surface, as shown in Fig. S2 .

The adsorption energy of each representative adsorbates on pristine SnO2 QDs is listed in Table 1 . The highest adsorption energy is −2.40 eV for CH3COOH while −1.87 eV for CH3SH ranks the second. The results show that the sulfide does not take priority in the competitive adsorption. Therefore, it is necessary to seek improvement for pristine SnO2 QDs on adsorption ability to sulfide.

Various metal dopants are attempted for modification by DFT simulations. As shown in Fig. S1 (b), one Sn atom is substituted by a metal atom to create the metal-doped SnO2 (110) matrix. Table 1 exhibits the adsorption energies of possible adsorbates on SnO2 (110) surfaces, which are modified by the metal impurities of Ni, Nb, Mo, Ag, Al and Cd. Compared with pristine SnO2, Nb modification raises Eads of CH3SH by 5%, and reduces Eads of CH3COOH by 55%. However, Eads of CH3NH2 increases by 18%, taking the highest among the five possible adsorbates. Thus, Nb modification changes the priority of adsorption to –NH2-containing species. For Mo-doped SnO2, Eads of CH3COOH decreases by 91%, but Eads of CH3SH also decreased by 9%. Moreover, the highest Eads of 1.76 eV takes place for CH3NH2 and CH3OH. Similarly, although Eads of CH3NH2 decreases by 52%, it is still the highest one on the Cd-SnO2 (110) surface, where CH3SH fails to take advantageous in adsorption. After Ag and Al incorporations, Eads of CH3SH reaches the highest among the five possible adsorbates. However, the values are 1.42 eV and 1.26 eV, 24% and 33% lower than ones on the pristine SnO2 (110) surface. Meanwhile, Ni modification provides the satisfactory adsorption properties. It not only increases Eads of CH3SH by 29% but also decreases Eads of CH3COOH by 10%, leaving the preferable adsorption for sulfide. After screening the adsorption of organics adsorption on metal-doped SnO2 supercells, the sulfide on Ni-doped SnO2 (110) has higher adsorption energy and electron transfer compared to the pristine SnO2. Therefore, the incorporation of Ni dopant would enhance the selectivity of SnO2 QDs towards sulfide and Ni would be a promising metal dopant to improve the sensing ability of SnO2 QDs for FSC detection.

Fig. S3 shows the band structures of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 supercells. The direct band gaps (Eg) of 4.31 eV and 4.09 eV are observed for pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2, respectively. Ni incorporation reduces the Eg of SnO2 and a donor energy level (ED) is observed at 1.91 eV below the conduction band edge (EC). Fig. S4 shows the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of state (PDOS) of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 supercells. By comparing their PDOS, the conduction band of Ni-SnO2 is composed of the 5 s orbits of Sn atoms while the valence band is contributed by the 2p orbits of O atoms. The donor energy level is provided by the 3d orbits of Ni atoms in the Ni-SnO2 matrix.

Fig. S5 shows the optimized adsorption positions of five possible adsorbates on the Ni-SnO2 (110) surface. The S atom of CH3SH combines with the 5-coordinate Sn1 to form a new Sn-S bond, and its length is 2.273 Å. At the same time, the S-H bond is destroyed, and the distance between H and O7 is reduced to 1.008 Å, forming a H-O bond. The Mulliken atomic populations of CH3SH, CH3COOH, CH3NH2, CH3OH, and CH3CN are listed in Table 2 . The Sn1, Sn2 and Ni1 atoms in semiconductor are positive charged, and the N, S and O atoms of adsorbates are negative before adsorption. After Ni incorporation, the charges of S atoms reverse positive. The charges of Ni atoms are reduced but is still positive compared with free adsorbates. CH3SH loses 0.25e in the SnO2 matrix while it loses 1.00e in the Ni-SnO2 supercell. It indicates a strong interaction of CH3SH with Ni-SnO2 (110) surface compared with pristine SnO2. Meanwhile, Eads of CH3SH is −2.42 eV, which is the highest among these organics. For CH3COOH adsorbate, double O atoms are adsorbed at Sn1 and Sn2 sites and the bond lengths are 2.175 Å and 1.986 Å, respectively. The H-O bond between of CH3COOH is broken and a new H-O bond is established between O6 from pristine or Ni-SnO2 and H from CH3COOH. CH3COOH accepts 0.14e from the pristine SnO2, whereas loses 0.14e to the Ni-SnO2 system. Thus, Eads of CH3COOH is reduced from −2.40 eV to −2.17 eV. The N atoms of CH3CN and CH3NH2 combine with the 5-coordinate Sn1 to form Sn-N bonds, which lead to the distortions of Sn-O bonds. The bridge-oxygen of SnO2 attracts H atoms of CH3NH2, but H-O bonds are not established. Both of nitrogen-containing organics donate electrons and are further positive charged. The O atom of CH3OH interacts with the 5-coordinate Sn1 with a new Sn-O bond of 1.911 Å formed. At the same time, the O–H bond of CH3OH is broken, and the distance between H and O6 is reduced to 1.025 Å, forming an H-O bond. In this case, CH3OH accepts 0.24e from the pristine SnO2, whereas loses 0.45e to the Ni-SnO2. In summary, the Ni incorporation changes the adsorption properties of five possible adsorbates by modifying their interaction with semiconductor surface. The Mulliken atomic population confirms that Ni modification enhances the adsorption ability to CH3SH by providing the most electron transfer and consequently enabling highest Eads. Hence, Ni dopant is selected after screening adsorption of organics on metal-doped SnO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental characterizations of pristine and Ni-modified SnO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs are shown in Fig. 2 . It is observed that crystal planes of (110), (101), (200) and (211) at 26.5°, 33.9°, 37.9° and 51.7°, respectively, in good agreement with the rutile tetragonal structure of SnO2 as JCPDS 00–041-1445 [27]. Slight shift in the peak position may attributed be tensile/compressive strain enhancement on the lattice after Ni incorporation [28]. The lattice parameters of SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs from XRD patterns are presented in Table S1 . Due to the similarity in ionic radii of 0.71 Å for Sn4+ and 0.69 Å for Ni2+, no significant variation in the lattice parameters is observed [29]. The average crystallite size is also calculated through the Debye-Scherrer formula. The calculation details of average crystallite size by the Debye-Scherrer formula are shown in Table S2 . The average crystallite size is found to be 5.83 nm for pristine SnO2 and 6.93 nm for Ni-SnO2 based on the (110), (101) and (211) diffraction peaks. The increase in crystallite size could be attributed to the reduction of strain and dislocations when the high Ni content is employed during hydrothermal process [29]. No additional peak is observed in Ni-SnO2, especially for the peaks of nickel oxides. It indicates that the Ni dopants are incorporated into the crystal matrix of SnO2, without any other phase formed.

Fig. 3
(a) and (b) show the TEM morphologies of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs. It is found that the Ni-SnO2 is in uniform spherical shape, which is similar to pristine SnO2. The d-spacing values are evaluated by using lattice plane fringes, which are approximate 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (110) planes of SnO2. The results above denote that the morphology of SnO2 QDs is hardly changed by Ni doping. The size distributions of both pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 are shown in Fig. S6 . The average grain size increases from 5.73 nm of pristine SnO2 to 6.87 nm of Ni-SnO2, which are close to the results from XRD analysis. The similar results of QD size from XRD and HRTEM demonstrate that most of QDs are crystallites without internal secondary grain boundaries. This conclusion is in accordance with previous works [30]. The HAADF-STEM images and elemental mappings are shown in Fig. 3 c-f. The elements of Ni, Sn and O show almost identical distribution, indicating that Ni atoms are of uniform distribution in the SnO2 matrix.

The superficial chemical states and compositions of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs are evaluated by XPS. Fig. 4
(a) shows the survey spectra, where the elements of Sn, O, and Ni are identified. In Fig. 4 (b), the high-resolution spectrum of Sn 3d shows double peaks at 495.6 and 487.1 eV, which are ascribed to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 of Sn4+ in SnO2, respectively [31]. Fan [32] conducted XPS analysis of Ni-SnO2 at four concentrations, namely 1.9%, 4.8%, 5.1%, and 8.6%. It was observed that the Sn 3d peaks of 1.9% and 4.8% Ni-SnO2 shifted to lower binding energy, while the Sn 3d peaks of 5.1% and 8.6% Ni-SnO2 shifted to higher binding energy. Additionally, Manikandan's [29] study on 7% Ni-SnO2 also exhibited a shift to higher binding energies for Sn 3d. Therefore, the peak shift to high binding energy in the present work is possible. The peaks of Sn 3d also shift slightly to high binding energies (495.7 and 487.3 eV) because of the Ni ions in the SnO2 lattice. Fig. 4 (c) shows the spectra of O 1 s, which are deconvoluted into double sub-peaks at 530.95 eV and 531.75 eV for SnO2 QDs, and the sub-peaks for Ni-SnO2 centered at 531.15 and 531.75 eV. The sub-peaks at 530.95 and 531.15 eV are assigned to lattice oxygen in SnO2 and Ni-SnO2. The sub-peaks at 531.75 eV are assigned to chemisorbed oxygen on SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 surfaces [33,34]. Similar to the Sn 3d peaks, the O 1 s peaks also shift towards high binding energy after Ni incorporation, indicating the construction of Ni-doped SnO2. Fig. 4 (d) reveals the high-resolution spectrum of Ni 2p in Ni-SnO2 QDs. According to the previous reports [29,35–37], the Ni 2p3/2 peak is centered around 856 eV and the Ni 2p1/2 peak is located near 874 eV, while the satellite peaks are observed around 861 eV. These results are consistent with the conclusions in previous reports. The energy difference between Ni 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks is 17.6 eV, smaller than the one of (18.4 eV) in NiO system [38]. Thus, it is proved that almost all of Ni ions substitute Sn atoms in crystal lattice, without any impurity phase of NiO. Moreover, the addition of Ni causes a slight shift of Ni peaks. The integrated peak areas are used to evaluate the elemental composition of Ni-SnO2 QDs, showing that the actual atomic Ni:Sn ratio is 3.9%, approximately half of Ni content used in preparation.

Fig. 5 shows the EDS spectra of the pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs. The elements of C, O, Sn, S and Cl are detected. S and Cl are the residues of CH4N2S and SnCl2 sources in the preparation process. The high-resolution local spectra for Ni in EDS have been shown in the inset of Fig. 5. The Ni peak for Ni-SnO2 is obvious compared to the baseline for pristine SnO2. Thus, the existence of Ni is confirmed by EDS spectra [39]. The Ni:Sn ratio is calculated to be 3.6%, close to the result from XPS analysis.

Compared to pristine SnO2, the UV–Vis absorption of the Ni-SnO2 QDs is significantly enhanced in the visible region above 400 nm, as shown in Fig. 6
(a). It could be ascribed to the impurity energy level in the band structure of Ni-SnO2 QDs [37]. The band gap is calculated by the Tauc relation, (αhν)2 = A(hν-Eg ), where α is the absorption coefficient, h is the planck constant, ν is the photon frequency and A is the coefficient. Thus, the band gaps of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs are 4.34 eV and 4.01 eV, respectively. It is noted that the calculated Eg of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs are 4.31 eV and 4.09 eV, which are close to these experimental results. Fig. 6 (b) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 QDs. The Ni-doped QDs shows a weak fluorescence intensity at 311 nm. It indicates that the Ni dopants prevent the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [40]. The variation in fluorescence emission spectra of Ni-SnO2 from different batches are shown in Fig. S7 . The average fluorescence intensity of Ni-SnO2 at 311 nm is 8549.43 ± 12.92 with a relative standard deviation of 0.15%. The coincidence of fluorescence spectra from different batches indicates the stability of the fluorescence properties of Ni-SnO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Ni-SnO2 QDs to FSC
ce:section.ce:para:
The Ni modification enhances the adsorption of sulfide on semiconductor and consequently improves the fluorescence response in FSC detection. By using the same experimental process, a calibration curve for FSC detection is established based on the Ni-SnO2 fluorescence probe. As shown in Fig. 7
(a), the fluorescence intensity of Ni-SnO2 QDs at 311 nm decreases gradually as FSC increases from 0.429 to 0.97%. Fig. 7 (b) plots the correlation of fluorescence response against FSC with the linear calibration curve. Thus, the Stern-Volmer equation for the linear correlation is concluded as Eq. (1).
(1)
F 0
F n
- 1 = 2.90915 + 1.26619
[ F S C ]

The slope of 1.26619 indicates good sensitivity of detection and the correlation coefficient R2 of 0.99509 denotes an excellent linear relationship. The standard deviation is 3.8% to 9.4% for detection of FSC by Ni-SnO2 (n = 5). The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.065% m/m by using LOD = 3δ/S, where δ is the standard deviation of intercept and S is the slope of the calibration curve. It is noted that the presence of substances other than sulfide may influence the fluorescence correlation of Ni-SnO2 due to the complex composition of fuel.

The recovery is a crucial indicator used to evaluate the actual detection of FSC. As shown in Table 3 , the recoveries are in the range of 94.75%–105.25%, meeting the criteria for acceptable recovery (n = 5). The results show that Ni-SnO2 QDs are feasible for actual sample detection. The ability of Ni-SnO2 QDs as fluorescent sensors for FSC detection is compared with other works in previous reports, as shown in Table 4 . The highly-sensitive and reliably-repeatable detection of FSC is accomplished based on Ni-doped SnO2 QDs, which can meet the detection requirements of FSC below 0.10% m/m within the ECAs.

It is found that sulfide lead to fluorescence quenching of Ni-SnO2 QDs during the FSC detection. The quenching mechanism can be categorized as static quenching [43], dynamic quenching [44], aggregation fluorescence quenching (ACQ) [45], inner filter effect (IFE) [46], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [47] and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [48]. In order to discuss the mechanism of sulfide-induced fluorescence quenching, the characterizations of fluorescence lifetimes, UV–Vis spectra, valence-band XPS spectra and fluorescence spectra are carried out. As shown in Fig. 8
(a), the fluorescence lifetimes of Ni-SnO2 QDs before and after exposure to diluted fuel are 10.51 ns and 6.04 ns, respectively. Fluorescence lifetime is significantly shortened by the addition of fuel. It infers that dynamic quenching dominates the fluorescence quenching of Ni-SnO2 QDs [14]. Fig. 8 (b) shows that UV–Vis spectra of Ni-SnO2 QDs with and without diluted fuel. Ni-modified QDs does not bring new absorption peaks after interaction with fuel, indicating that no non-fluorescent ground-state complex is formed [49]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching may be ascribed to FRET or PET. The UV absorbance spectra of fuel and fluorescent spectra of Ni-SnO2 QDs are plotted for comparison in Fig. 8 (c). The absorption peaks of diluted fuel are at 209 nm, 225 nm and 259 nm while the fluorescence emission peak of Ni-SnO2 QDs locates at 311 nm, which do not overlap the absorption peaks above. FRET has the feature that the emission spectrum of QDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of quencher [14]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism could be PET other than FRET in the detection of FSC by Ni-SnO2 QDs.

The mechanism of PET has the characteristic LUMO and HOMO between the SnO2 QDs and the quencher [14]. Therefore, the band structures of semiconductors as well as the organic adsorbates are beneficial to reveal the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The HOMO and LUMO of organics are calculated by Gaussian 09 package [50]. Table 5 shows the HOMO/LUMO positions for various organics in the ethanol solution with respect to the NHE. For semiconductors, the positions of valence band edge (EV) are calculated by the XPS valence band spectra as shown in Fig. 8 (d). The EV positions of pristine and Ni-modified SnO2 QDs are 4.15 eV and 4.05 eV, respectively. EC positions are obtained by EC = EV- Eg [18]. Fig. S8 shows the band edge positions of pristine SnO2 and Ni-SnO2 as well as HOMO/LUMO of the organics. As HOMO of organics are located in the band gap of semiconductor, the organics act as donors and provide additional electrons, which transfer to semiconductor QDs to capture photogenerated holes [51], leading to the separation of electron–hole pairs as well as to the quenching of fluorescence emission. Therefore, the mechanism of sulfide-induced fluorescence quenching is ascribed to the PET principle.

Therefore, the fluorescent sensor by Ni-SnO2 is designed and the FSC detection with advantages of fast response, high selectivity, reliable repeatability and low cost is accomplished based on the mechanism of PET principle. Portable devices are expected based on this novel technique and they will be beneficial to the pollution control as well as marine authorities.

As pristine SnO2 QDs are not able to establish a calibration curve for FSC detection, Ni is selected after screening the DFT calculations to modify the properties of SnO2 QDs, which are used as a fluorescent sensor. The fluorescence sensing mechanism is further discussed. Several conclusions are drawn:
i.
The adsorption energy of each adsorbates on metal-doped SnO2 QDs are calculated and screened based on DFT computation. Compared to the pristine SnO2, the sulfide on Ni-doped SnO2 (110) has higher adsorption energy and more electron transfer. The incorporation of Ni dopant enhances the selectivity of SnO2 QDs towards sulfide.
ii.
A fluorescence sensor based on Ni-SnO2 QDs is designed and constructed for FSC detection. A good linear relationship is observed between the FSC and fluorescence emission of Ni-SnO2 QDs with a range of 0.429–0.97 % m/m (R2 = 0.99509) and with the LOD of 0.065% m/m, which is able to meet the detection requirements of FSC below 0.10% m/m within the ECAs.
iii.
The fluorescence lifetime, UV–Vis spectra, fluorescence spectra and HOMO/LUMO of organics demonstrate that sulfide-induced fluorescence quenching of Ni-SnO2 QDs is ascribed to the PET. The sulfide in fuel as donors provide electrons to capture the photogenerated holes of Ni-SnO2 QDs, leading to the separation of electron-hole pairs as well as fluorescence quenching.

Jianqiao Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wenping Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hongjin Li: Data curation, Methodology, Validation. Yanan Zhang: Investigation. Zhaoxia Zhai: Investigation. Yang Wang: Investigation. Ce Fu: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Junsheng Wang: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
